project_overview.project_name: Building Skills for Human Capital Development in South Sudan
project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase skills development opportunities in teaching and digital agriculture and strengthen capacity for management of the education system
project_overview.total_financing: US$56.43 million (US$45 million IDA grant of which US$10 million from Window for Host Communities and Refugees, plus US$11.43 million from Global Partnership for Education)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Over 400,000 school-age children expected to benefit from improved teaching quality; 11,000 teachers (out of 31,000 unqualified) to receive professional development; 3,500 youth to receive digital agriculture training; approximately 6,000 refugees and host community members including 5,000 children in re-operationalized schools and 1,000 teachers in refugee-hosting areas

geographic_scope.coverage_type: National with specific focus on refugee-hosting areas
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes - South Sudan is classified as a fragile and conflict-affected state. Project operates in context of protracted civil war with repeated cycles of violence. Violence remains elevated in certain areas including Upper Nile, Jonglei, Western Equatoria, Central Equatoria States, and Greater Tonj/Warrap State/Abyei area. However, signing of Revitalized Agreement in September 2018 and formation of unity government in February 2020 has provided some stability. Project specifically targets refugee-hosting areas and internally displaced populations.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Teaching Skills to Strengthen Education Delivery
key_components.description: Establish scalable teacher professional development system through: (1.1) pre-service and in-service training for 10,000 teachers with focus on structured pedagogy, content knowledge, language skills, climate change awareness; (1.2) specialized training for 1,000 refugee and host community teachers including English courses and psychosocial support modules; (1.3) accelerated secondary education for 5,000 unqualified teachers; (1.4) rapid evaluations of training programs. Includes rehabilitation of National Teacher Training Institutions (NTTIs), scholarships for female teachers, and nationwide communication campaigns.
key_components.financing: US$29 million (US$20 million National PBA, US$3 million WHR, US$6 million GPE)

key_components.description: Develop and implement innovative digital agriculture skills program through blended learning model combining remote training from international experts with local tutors. Training in precision and climate-resilient agriculture for approximately 3,500 youth across 10 public universities and polytechnics. Includes curriculum development, institution renovations, trainer recruitment and training, stipends for disadvantaged students (targeting 500 refugee/host community youth and 35% female participation).
key_components.financing: US$8 million (US$6 million PBA, US$2 million WHR)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Inclusion of Refugee and Host Communities
key_components.description: Re-operationalize 40 non-functional schools in refugee-hosting areas (Ruweng, Upper Nile, Western Equatoria, Central Equatoria) to serve approximately 5,000 out-of-school children. Includes: (3.1) refurbishment and equipment of school facilities with climate-resilient design and gender-separated WASH facilities; (3.2) school grants for operational expenditures including teaching materials and dignity kits; (3.3) community partnership model for school management with parent involvement; (3.4) recruitment of qualified teachers (particularly female) with government payroll transition after 2-3 years.
key_components.financing: US$8 million (US$5 million WHR, US$3 million GPE)

key_components.description: Strengthen government capacity for education system management through: (4.1) institutional capacity assessments and development plans at national and sub-national levels, teacher policy development, digital teacher management system; (4.2) strengthening Education Management Information System (EMIS) with improved data collection, web-based platform, real-time data integration; (4.3) project management including PIU establishment, staff training on climate change adaptation and disaster preparedness.
key_components.financing: US$11.43 million (US$9 million PBA, US$2.43 million GPE)

key_components.description: Zero-allocation CERC to allow rapid reallocation of uncommitted funds for emergency response to political, social, economic uncertainties or natural disasters affecting service delivery. Includes procedures for triggering and implementing CERC.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of General Education and Instruction (MoGEI) and Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Technology (MoHEST). MoGEI implements Components 1 and 3; MoHEST implements Component 2; both jointly implement Component 4. Each ministry establishes separate Project Implementation Units (PIUs) with Project Coordinator, Financial Management Specialist, Accountant, Procurement Specialist, Environmental Specialist, Social Specialist, and Gender-Based Violence specialist.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) co-chaired by Under Secretaries of MoGEI and MoHEST, comprising representatives from State Education Administration Bodies, Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA), Ministry of Finance, UNHCR, and National Education Coalition (NEC). PSC provides strategic direction, high-level risk management, and decision-making. Third-Party Monitoring (TPM) agent reports directly to PSC and verifies project results on sample basis bi-annually. Partnership with Galilee International Management Institute (GIMI) for digital agriculture program delivery.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: All procurement follows World Bank Procurement Regulations (July 1, 2016, revised November 2017 and August 2020) and Anti-Corruption Guidelines. Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) prepared by both ministries. Procurement Plan updated every 6 months. Use of Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement (STEP) system for all contracts. Prior review by World Bank for high-risk/complex contracts; post-review for others. National procurement procedures may be used when approaching domestic vendors, subject to World Bank provisions. Experienced Procurement Consultants hired in PIUs.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: M&E responsibility with PIUs at MoGEI and MoHEST. M&E officers hired as core PIU team members. Regular supervision of implementation sites, biannual progress reports, assessment and evaluation studies, citizen engagement surveys. Use of Geo-Enabled Initiative for Monitoring and Supervision (GEMS) for remote monitoring and real-time risk monitoring. Third-party monitoring on sample basis for data verification. Integrated monitoring system designed to capture data for all Results Framework indicators. Data verification through independent audit of ministry accounts and cross-referencing with EMIS data. Annual external audit by National Audit Chamber (NAC). Quarterly Interim Unaudited Financial Reports (IFRs) submitted to World Bank within 45 days of quarter end.

identified_risks.risk_description: Repeated cycles of conflict threaten fragile peace agreement reached in 2018. Country's political landscape marked by shifting power dynamics that threaten resurgence of national-level political conflict and violence. Delayed realization of peace process implementation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design ensures access to training opportunities in all 10 states. Thorough security risk assessment and management plan to be developed prior to effectiveness. Project's limited focus on key sectors and skills creates clear opportunities for improved human development outcomes, reducing likelihood of generating additional political risks. Project prioritizes refugee and host communities, aligned with South Sudan's progressive refugee policy, not expected to increase political risk.

identified_risks.risk_description: South Sudanese economy highly vulnerable to external shocks (COVID-19, climate events, food prices, locust outbreak). High risk of debt distress, possibility of further contraction due to declining oil production, lingering climate shocks, and stagnating poverty. Education sector mostly externally funded.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Bulk of Project activities financed by World Bank and partners, mitigating impact of macroeconomic risks on achievement of objectives. Digital agriculture training component has potential to build resilience of agriculture sector to natural disasters and economic shocks from agricultural disruptions.

identified_risks.risk_description: Education sector has relied on external financing and humanitarian assistance, resulting in fragmented programs and lack of coordination among key actors. Sector lacks coherent implementation strategy to address low quality and high inefficiency. Limited planning capacity of MoGEI.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project incorporates both MoGEI and MoHEST in governance and implementation to ensure shared ownership and accountability. Focus on building capacity and leadership within Government to implement key education programs coherently across country. World Bank will leverage convening ability to bring together development and humanitarian partners with Government authorities to foster greater coherence. Government's Refugee Act 2012 provides inclusive legal framework for refugee inclusion; reversal of policy positions not expected during implementation.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Low institutional capacity in country to achieve national education goals. Limited skilled human resources to execute and monitor education programs, maintain and strengthen education systems, coordinate with stakeholders, and ensure technical and financial credibility in budget allocation. Weak capacity at sub-national levels.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project focuses on encouraging leadership and coordination through cross-ministerial PSC including MoGEI and MoHEST authorities. Provides TA and capacity building on key aspects of implementation and system oversight, including sector data collection and fiduciary management. Institutional capacity assessments at national and sub-national levels to identify key constraints. Citizen engagement indicator and Grievance Redress Mechanism to ensure feedback and timely grievance resolution. Project design incorporates building systems and institutions for continued programs beyond project lifespan. Specific efforts to engage refugee and host community committees in design and implementation.

identified_risks.risk_description: Institutional capacity for fiduciary management is limited. MoGEI and MoHEST have significant capacity gaps including inadequate budget monitoring systems, lack of adequate accounting capacity, failure to prepare annual financial statements, weak internal audit, and weak internal control arrangements. No experience with World Bank-financed projects (MoHEST). Limited procurement capacity and experience.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: FM arrangements discharged from PIUs within ministries. FM section headed by consultant Financial Management Specialists supported by project accountants and designated ministry accountants. Three Financial Management Consultants to be hired within 3 months of effectiveness. Designated Account opened in acceptable commercial bank. Accounting software (QuickBooks) to be acquired within 4 months of effectiveness. Regular FM trainings for project staff. Project FM manual to provide guidance on FM functions. Strict fiduciary control mechanisms and application of World Bank FM rules built into design. Quarterly IFRs submitted within 45 days of quarter end. Annual external audit by NAC. Regular internal audit oversight and World Bank FM supervision reviews. Quarterly review of Statements of Expenditure. TPM every 6 months. In-depth FM review every 2 years. Approved and authorized attendance sheets required to support teacher salary payments. Dedicated civil servants deployed to PIUs for knowledge transfer and continuous procurement trainings.

identified_risks.risk_description: Environmental risks: Minor civil works (rehabilitation of NTTIs, renovation of institutions, equipment procurement) may generate occupational health and safety, pollution, waste, and electronic waste risks. Potential exposure to environmental liabilities (asbestos, lead-based paint). Social risks: Contextual risks from conflict and fragility, security and safety risks to beneficiaries and workers, weak institutions especially at local level, accessibility challenges in remote areas. Project-specific risks: inadequate stakeholder consultation, selection biases and corrupt practices, lack of functional grievance mechanisms, discriminatory practices, inadequate labor protection, labor influx, gender-based violence/sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA), conflict between hosts and refugees over resource sharing.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental: Small, localized, reversible nature of environmental impacts. Detailed Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) and Labor Management Plan (LMP) prepared. Energy-efficient architectural designs for building rehabilitation including renewable energy sources (solar, wind), rainwater harvesting, water recycling, drainage improvement for flood control, weather-resistant materials. Climate-safe techniques and accessibility for students with special needs. Careful supervision to avoid accidents. Social: Detailed ESF instruments including Security Risk Assessment and Management Plan, Labor Management Plan, GBV Assessment and Action Plan (preparation of these instruments are disbursement conditions for non-TA activities). PIUs hire Environment, Social and GBV Specialists to monitor risks and ensure mitigation implementation. Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) disclosed March 21, 2023, includes accessibility arrangements for people with disabilities. Anti-sexual harassment policies and codes of conduct at all participating institutions with fair, accessible, transparent complaints mechanisms ensuring confidentiality. Anti-sexual harassment and gender sensitivity training for all enrolled students and trainers. Community mobilization and involvement in refugee-hosting areas. Merit-based selection processes for institutions and beneficiaries. Grievance Redress Mechanism established within 2 months of effectiveness. Third-party monitoring to verify selection processes. Community oversight of teacher attendance and school management. Accessibility accommodations for people with disabilities including diversified notification means, assistive language tools, accessible facilities. Teacher training modules include considerations for learners with disabilities. School renovations meet accessibility requirements.

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential risks related to refugee protection framework adequacy and implementation of refugee rights. Implementation of refugee rights hindered by capacity limitations of Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA) and other government institutions, and country's challenging socioeconomic environment.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: World Bank, in close consultation with UNHCR, has confirmed adequacy of South Sudan's refugee protection framework (assessment dated August 31, 2022). South Sudan has one of East Africa's more progressive refugee legal and policy frameworks granting refugees rights to move, settle, work, and benefit from public services. Little risk of backsliding on government's strong policy and longstanding demonstrated commitment to refugee protection. World Bank working with government to support further reforms benefiting refugees and hosts through policy dialogue in context of IDA WHR. Government has made important progress since WHR eligibility: allocated 4,000 hectares of land to refugees for agricultural cultivation, contributed textbooks to schools serving refugees, finalized Durable Solutions Strategy and Plan of Action for Refugees/IDPs/Returnees/Host Communities, finalized 'Four Freedoms' agreement with Sudan. Project includes specific components (Component 3 and Subcomponent 1.2) targeting refugee and host community education needs.

identified_risks.risk_description: High technical complexity of delivering teacher training across South Sudan with limited government systems and lack of data on effective delivery approaches. Uncertainty about most cost-effective delivery models.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design includes flexibility to allow for adjustment during implementation. Small-scale pilots to test key aspects of design and implementation including teaching materials and training models. Rapid evaluations (process evaluations and randomized controlled trials) to shed light on what models work better and maximize impact. Subcomponent 1.4 dedicated to evaluation of teacher professional development programs with multiple design iterations. Project allows flexibility in structured pedagogy design to test what works better in South Sudan context.

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential for inadequate stakeholder consultation and engagement, particularly with vulnerable groups, refugees, and host communities. Risk of selection biases in beneficiary identification.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) prepared and disclosed March 21, 2023. Project undertook stakeholder consultations during preparation including with vulnerable groups (women, refugees, host communities). Timely dissemination of project documents in English and key local languages using multiple platforms (mail, community radio, mobile public address systems). Citizen engagement indicator in Results Framework with participant satisfaction surveys for digital agriculture program. Feedback from surveys used to fine-tune training and identify additional support needed. Grievance Redress Mechanism with grievance redress committee co-chaired by PIU directors including environment and social specialists. Community mobilization and involvement in refugee-hosting areas in school placement, refurbishment, and management. Merit-based selection processes for institutions and beneficiaries with transparent criteria. Third-party monitoring to verify selection processes and prevent corrupt practices.

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS4)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (ESS7)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) to be established within 2 months of effectiveness. Structure and terms of reference satisfactory to World Bank. Grievance redress committee co-chaired by PIU directors and including environment and social specialists. Fair, accessible, transparent complaints mechanism ensuring confidentiality and security. Project-level GRM for communities and individuals adversely affected. World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available for complaints not resolved at project level. World Bank's Accountability Mechanism (AM) including Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service available for complaints after concerns brought to Bank Management.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender focus throughout project. Women in South Sudan face particularly difficult conditions due to traditional patriarchal norms and conflict consequences, with gender-based violence levels among highest in world. Only 18% of teachers are female; only 26% of TVET students are female; 84% of women are illiterate; 50% of girls under 18 are married. Project aims to increase female teacher share from 26% to 35% through: targeted information campaigns to encourage female enrollment in pre-service training; professional development for female volunteer teachers; scholarships for selected prospective and volunteer female teachers; nationwide communication campaign; anti-sexual harassment policies and codes of conduct at all institutions; anti-sexual harassment and gender sensitivity training for all students and trainers; fair, accessible, transparent complaints mechanisms. For digital agriculture training, project aims to increase female enrollment from 26% to 35% through: testing different enrollment encouragement approaches; providing information on labor market returns; connecting participants with female mentors/role models; stipends for female students; peer groups for female participants to develop networks and socioemotional skills; anti-sexual harassment policies and training. Component 3 prioritizes recruitment of female teachers for re-operationalized schools. All participating institutions required to have clearly defined, strongly worded, readily accessible anti-sexual harassment policies.
vulnerable_groups: Refugees (308,374 as of December 31, 2022, with 94% Sudanese, 3.5% DRC, remainder from Ethiopia, CAR, Burundi; 52% female; 81% women and children; 60% under 18)
Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) - approximately 2 million displaced during conflict
Host communities in refugee-hosting areas (often in isolated areas with limited government services and market opportunities)
Women and girls (84% illiterate; 50% under 18 married; fewer employment opportunities; high gender-based violence)
Youth (large population with average age 19; 45% under 15; limited employment opportunities)
Volunteer teachers (46% of primary teachers; mostly without training; limited qualifications)
People with disabilities and special educational needs (barriers include social stigma, absence of accessibility accommodations, cultural beliefs about limited productive roles)
Female-headed households (slightly more than one-quarter of all households due to conflict)
Persons affected by food insecurity (6.6 million experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for FCV context. Incorporates lessons from World Bank's FCV Strategy (2020-2025) emphasizing importance of strengthening legitimacy and capacity of core institutions. Project focuses on building government institutional capacity and coordination between tiers of government. Involves local stakeholders in implementation through state and county authorities collaboration. Project recognizes that education is critical for building social cohesion and stability - evidence shows countries investing in human capital create more engaged citizens forming foundation for stable democracies. Teacher training and skills development expected to increase opportunity cost of violence and reduce susceptibility to recruitment into armed groups. Project design includes flexibility and rapid evaluations to adjust to volatile and rapidly changing context. Incorporates lessons from implementation of South Sudan Safety Net Project showing importance of flexibility in design and implementation to respond to shocks (lockdowns, floods, conflict). Project includes Contingency Emergency Response Component (CERC) to allow rapid reallocation of funds for emergency response. Security Risk Assessment and Management Plan required prior to effectiveness. Third-party monitoring to verify implementation in insecure areas. Use of remote monitoring tools (GEMS) to overcome access constraints due to security and logistics. Project prioritizes refugee and host communities, recognizing their particular vulnerabilities and displacement trauma.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: South Sudan is classified as fragile and conflict-affected state. Key fragility factors: (1) Prolonged civil war with repeated cycles of violence - 380,000 deaths reported between 2013-2018; 2 million internally displaced; 2.3 million refugees abroad. (2) Delayed peace process implementation - Revitalized Agreement signed September 2018, unity government formed February 2020, but implementation remains sluggish. (3) Continued violence in certain areas - Upper Nile, Jonglei, Western Equatoria, Central Equatoria States, Greater Tonj, Warrap State/Abyei area. (4) Severe economic collapse - household incomes declined 70% between 2011-2018; 9 out of 10 people live in poverty; negative growth expected 2023. (5) Weak institutions - limited institutional capacity for service delivery, planning, coordination, fiduciary management, especially at sub-national levels. (6) Limited human capital - education severely disrupted; 26% of schools non-operational due to lack of teachers; 94% of school children unable to read simple text by age 10; high pupil-qualified teacher ratio (86:1); 46% of primary teachers are untrained volunteers. (7) Food insecurity - 6.6 million experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity; agricultural productivity severely affected by conflict and climate shocks. (8) Climate vulnerability - 5th most vulnerable country to climate change; 8th most affected by weather-related loss events; 4 years of historic flooding; droughts. (9) Gender-based violence - among highest in world; 50% of girls under 18 married. (10) Refugee and IDP crisis - 308,374 refugees hosted; 2 million IDPs; host communities experiencing service delivery gaps. Project directly addresses these fragility factors through human capital development, institutional capacity building, and support to vulnerable populations.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project operates in highly insecure environment with ongoing conflict and violence. Security risks include: (1) Protracted civil war with repeated cycles of violence threatening fragile peace agreement. (2) Elevated violence in certain areas despite overall decline since peace agreement. (3) Attacks on schools and military use of schools during conflict period (293 attacks reported 2013-2017). (4) Accessibility challenges - narrow window of opportunity (6 months dry season) when most of country accessible; significant parts of country inaccessible due to security and logistics. (5) Sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA) and sexual harassment risks, particularly for women and girls. (6) Arbitrary arrest and detention of refugees and asylum seekers (though incidents have slightly decreased). (7) Pushback of persons of concern at southern border points. (8) Gender-based violence risks and reported incidents facing refugees not reduced. Mitigation measures: (1) Security Risk Assessment and Management Plan required prior to effectiveness. (2) Use of remote monitoring tools (GEMS) to enable supervision despite access constraints. (3) Third-party monitoring to verify implementation on sample basis. (4) Project design ensures access to training opportunities in all 10 states to reduce concentration of risk. (5) Anti-sexual harassment policies, codes of conduct, and complaints mechanisms at all institutions. (6) GBV Assessment and Action Plan prepared. (7) PIUs hire GBV specialists to monitor and address risks. (8) Community involvement and oversight mechanisms. (9) Flexibility in project design to adjust to security changes. (10) Coordination with UNHCR and CRA for refugee protection. (11) Environmental and social specialists in PIUs to monitor security and safety risks.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - significant displacement and refugee populations. South Sudan hosts 308,374 refugees as of December 31, 2022 (64,225 refugee households), with over 90% located in Jamjang (Ruweng Administrative Area) and Bunj Town (Upper Nile State). Refugee composition: 289,840 Sudanese (94%), 10,944 DRC (3.5%), remainder from Ethiopia, CAR, Burundi. Demographics: 52% female; 81% women and children; 60% under 18; 38% between 18-59; remainder over 60. Additionally, approximately 2 million internally displaced persons (IDPs) within South Sudan. Host communities in refugee-hosting areas often live in isolated areas with limited government services and market opportunities, experiencing significant service delivery gaps. Project specifically targets these populations through: (1) Component 3 dedicated to inclusion of refugee and host communities - re-operationalizing 40 schools in refugee-hosting areas (Ruweng, Upper Nile, Western Equatoria, Central Equatoria) to serve approximately 5,000 out-of-school children. (2) Subcomponent 1.2 supporting training for 1,000 refugee and host community teachers with specialized modules addressing their needs including English courses and psychosocial support for trauma. (3) Component 2 targeting at least 500 refugee and host community youth for digital agriculture training with stipends. (4) US$10 million from Window for Host Communities and Refugees (WHR) financing. (5) Coordination with UNHCR and Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA) for implementation. (6) Government's progressive refugee policy framework - Refugee Act 2012 grants refugees rights to move, settle, work, benefit from public services; acceded to 1951 Refugee Convention and 1967 Protocol; adopted draft East African Community Refugee Management Policy. (7) Government has allocated 4,000 hectares of land to refugees for agricultural cultivation; contributed textbooks to schools serving refugees; finalized Durable Solutions Strategy and Plan of Action. (8) Project recognizes education as durable solution for refugee and IDP children; training refugee teachers enhances employment opportunities and increases pool of teachers who can teach in native languages refugees speak; improved skills in agriculture increases IDP employment opportunities and reduces devastating losses in earnings and labor market connections.
